question: Compare the Ch.01 plant-level description with Ch.05 module-level thermal-hydraulics using Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, and Figure 5.1-2.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, and waste management. It explains the distinction between certified and site-specific design elements, noting that certain conceptual design information is included for context but is not part of the certified design. The section also references figures illustrating the plant layout and system boundaries, and notes how security-related information is handled.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It lists the relevant diagrams and legends used for both I&C and piping and instrumentation diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design elements provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant. Additionally, it notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters for both primary and secondary systems, such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures. These parameters are calculated using thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand, with uncertainties and design margins considered to ensure efficient operation and safety. The design supports natural circulation with low pressure losses, and uncertainties like SG tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow determination.

nuscale_ch01_0767.title: DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System Malfunction That Increases Reactor Coolant InventoryII.4.B Input Parameters and Initial Conditions - Scram Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0767.summary: This section confirms that the NuScale design meets the regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2 regarding chemical and volume control system malfunctions that increase reactor coolant inventory, with no exceptions noted for scram characteristics, initial power level, or core burnup. It also clarifies that certain SRP guidance related to pressurizer relief valves is not applicable to NuScale, as the design does not use power-operated relief valves but instead uses spring-loaded safety valves, and any related mechanical failure is addressed elsewhere.

nuscale_ch01_0059.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Fluid-Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0059.summary: The section describes thermal-hydraulic performance testing of the NuScale steam generator using an electrically-heated facility at SIET in Italy. The tests, which included various heating zones and types of experiments, allowed precise control and measurement of heat flux and pressure dynamics to support design validation, code benchmarking, and regulatory inspection. The results informed steam generator design aspects such as flow restrictor sizing and assessment of flow-induced vibration risks.

nuscale_ch01_0058.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Electrically Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0058.summary: This section describes the critical heat flux (CHF) testing conducted for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design using an electrically heated 5x5 simulated fuel bundle with AREVA HTP™/HMP™ grid technology. The tests aimed to obtain CHF data, validate and develop CHF correlations, and collect thermal-hydraulic performance data under various operating conditions, with experiments performed at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics facility in Germany.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes special plant features of the NuScale Power Plant, highlighting structures such as the Central Utility Building and Diesel Generator Buildings, which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and equipment. It also emphasizes human factors engineering, noting that the plant design minimizes human error through automation, digital controls, and optimized workload management, in compliance with regulatory requirements and industry best practices.

nuscale_ch01_0715.title: DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0: Loss of External Load; Turbine Trip; Loss of Condenser Vacuum; Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve; and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed)II.3.D Values of Parameters Used in Analytical Model - Instrumentation Setpoints for Mitigating System Actuation
nuscale_ch01_0715.summary: This section of the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report confirms that the analytical models for various turbine and steam system transients (such as loss of external load, turbine trip, and others) conform to regulatory criteria, specifically DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5. It states that the values of parameters used in the models—including instrumentation setpoints for mitigating system actuation—meet the required standards, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation, eliminating the need for pumps or external piping, and is integrated within each NuScale Power Module (NPM). The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat from the reactor core, maintain proper coolant chemistry, and ensure uniform boron concentration, supporting both normal and off-normal plant conditions. These design features collectively ensure the safety and performance of each NPM.

nuscale_ch01_0766.title: DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System Malfunction That Increases Reactor Coolant InventoryII.4.A Input Parameters and Initial Conditions - Initial Power Level
nuscale_ch01_0766.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for chemical and volume control system malfunctions that increase reactor coolant inventory. It states that NuScale meets the requirements for initial power level, scram characteristics, and core burnup, but notes that guidance related to power-operated relief valves is only partially applicable since NuScale uses spring-loaded safety valves instead. Any mechanical failure of these valves is addressed in a separate analysis.

nuscale_ch01_0768.title: DSRS 15.5.1-15.5.2, Rev 0: Chemical and Volume Control System Malfunction That Increases Reactor Coolant InventoryII.4.C Input Parameters and Initial Conditions - Core Burnup
nuscale_ch01_0768.summary: This section documents NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Chemical and Volume Control System malfunction that increases reactor coolant inventory, specifically addressing input parameters and initial conditions such as core burnup. It states that NuScale’s design conforms to the relevant DSRS sections, and clarifies that certain SRP guidance regarding pressurizer relief valves is not applicable due to NuScale’s use of spring-loaded safety valves instead of power-operated relief valves. The section also references conformance tables summarizing these regulatory alignments.

edges.description: Section A provides a detailed module-level description of the reactor coolant system, which specifies and elaborates on the general plant-level systems overviewed in Section B, with both sections connected through shared system components and design criteria referenced in Table 1.2-1 and Table 5.1-2.

Section A provides a summary description of the reactor coolant system's design basis at the module level, while Section B references the use of detailed drawings and figures (such as Table 1.2-1) to visually clarify and support the plant-level descriptions found in Section A.

Section A provides a detailed module-level description of the reactor coolant system, which is one of the systems identified in Section B as part of the certified plant-level design interfaces, with both sections referencing the structures, systems, and components listed in Table 1.2-1.

Section A provides the design basis and general description of the reactor coolant system (RCS), while Section B evaluates the system's performance by establishing steady-state parameter values within the design basis described in Section A.

Section A provides a high-level overview of the plant's systems and design criteria, while Section B offers detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and specify the systems described in Section A.

Section A provides a general description of the plant and identifies the structures, systems, and components included in the certified design, which Section B then uses as the basis for specifying the interface requirements between the certified design and the site-specific design in the COL application.

Section A provides a general overview of the plant's systems, including cooling and safety features, while Section B offers detailed thermal-hydraulic performance criteria and system evaluations for specific modules, with both sections connected through the design values and operational characteristics summarized in Table 1.2-1 and Table 5.1-2.

Section A provides detailed drawings and figures that visually clarify plant-level design information, which supports the interface requirements and boundaries described in Section B by illustrating the certified design components referenced there.

Section A provides detailed drawings and figures (including piping and instrumentation diagrams) that visually clarify the plant-level design described in Chapter 1, while Section B evaluates the thermal-hydraulic performance of specific modules (such as the steam generator and decay heat removal system) at the module level, with both sections referencing and relying on shared design data from Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, and Figure 5.1-2 to ensure consistency between plant-level descriptions and module-level analyses.

Section A provides the plant-level boundaries and identification of certified systems, which are further detailed at the module level in Section B's thermal-hydraulic system evaluation, with both sections referencing specific tables and figures (e.g., Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, Figure 5.1-2) to connect plant-wide descriptions to module-specific performance parameters.

Section A specifies initial conditions and scram characteristics for plant-level accident analysis, which must align with the module-level reactor coolant system design described in Section B to ensure that the thermal-hydraulic behavior under accident scenarios is accurately represented.

Section A specifies detailed input parameters and initial conditions (such as scram characteristics and initial power level) for module-level thermal-hydraulic analyses, which are derived from and must be consistent with the general plant-level descriptions and design criteria provided in Section B.

Section A specifies initial conditions and scram characteristics for accident analysis at the plant level, while Section B provides detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify and support the understanding of these initial conditions and system responses at the module level, with both sections connected through the use of design parameters and system configurations referenced in the analysis tables and figures.

Section A describes the specific thermal-hydraulic performance testing of the steam generator at the module level, providing detailed test methods and boundary conditions, while Section B provides the plant-level design basis for the reactor coolant system, with the connection being that the module-level test results in A are intended to validate or support the plant-level system performance described in B.

Section A provides detailed technical information about steam generator thermal-hydraulic testing that specifies and supports the general plant-level descriptions and operating characteristics outlined in Section B.

Section A describes the thermal-hydraulic testing methodology for the steam generator, while Section B provides the piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify the system configuration referenced in the testing, connecting the experimental setup to the plant's design documentation.

Section A provides specific thermal-hydraulic test data and CHF correlation development for the NuScale fuel design, which supports the general plant-level design basis and operational description of the reactor coolant system in Section B by ensuring the fuel's thermal performance aligns with system requirements.

Section A provides detailed thermal-hydraulic testing results and fuel design data that support and specify the general plant-level descriptions and performance claims outlined in Section B, particularly regarding fuel and safety system performance.

Section A provides test data and correlations for fuel thermal-hydraulic performance, which inform the design details depicted in Section B's piping and instrumentation diagrams by ensuring the depicted systems can support validated operational parameters.

Section A provides a plant-level overview of structures like the Central Utility Building that support general plant operations, while Section B details the module-level design basis of the reactor coolant system, with both sections connected through Table 1.2-1 and Table 5.1-2, which map plant-wide systems to specific module functions and interfaces.

Section A provides specific details about the Central Utility Building and its functions, which are part of the broader auxiliary systems and plant features generally described in Section B's summary of the overall facility.

Section A provides a general description of plant-level features and utilities, while Section B offers detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and specify the design and operation of those same plant systems, connecting general descriptions to specific technical details.

Section A specifies the analytical model parameters and instrumentation setpoints at the plant level for transient events, while Section B provides the module-level design basis and summary description of the reactor coolant system, with the plant-level parameters in A needing to be consistent with the module-level system described in B.

Section A specifies analytical model parameter values and instrumentation setpoints for transient events, which are detailed applications of the general plant systems and design features described in Section B.

Section A specifies the analytical model parameters and instrumentation setpoints used for safety analysis at the plant level, while Section B provides the detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually represent and clarify the instrumentation and control systems referenced in Section A.

Section A specifies the initial plant-level input parameters and scram characteristics required for accident analysis, while Section B provides module-level thermal-hydraulic test data that supports and validates those input parameters and assumptions.

Section A specifies initial plant-level conditions such as scram characteristics and power level, which are necessary prerequisites for interpreting the module-level thermal-hydraulic performance and critical heat flux testing described in Section B.

Section A specifies the regulatory requirements and initial conditions for analyzing chemical and volume control system malfunctions, while Section B provides the plant-level description of systems (such as chemical treatment equipment) that are relevant to those initial conditions, connecting the general plant features to the specific analysis parameters.

Section A provides initial conditions and scram characteristics at the plant level, which are prerequisites for the module-level instrumentation setpoints and analytical model parameters described in Section B, as both sections use initial power levels and system responses that must be consistent for integrated safety analysis.

Section A describes thermal-hydraulic testing of steam generator tubes using fluid heating to establish boundary conditions, while Section B details critical heat flux testing for the fuel design, both providing experimental data that inform and validate plant-level and module-level thermal-hydraulic analyses as referenced in the analysis question.

Section A details the thermal-hydraulic testing methodology for the steam generator at the module level, while Section B provides a general plant-level description of utility structures, with both sections connected through the plant's overall design and operational context as summarized in Table 1.2-1 and Table 5.1-2, which map plant features to module-specific performance data.

Section A describes the thermal-hydraulic testing methods and boundary conditions for the steam generator, which provide the empirical basis for the parameter values and initial conditions used in the analytical models referenced in Section B for evaluating system response to transient events.

Section A provides detailed thermal-hydraulic performance testing data for the NuScale fuel design, which supports the plant-level description in Section B by supplying technical evidence relevant to the operation and safety of systems housed in the Central Utility Building as described in the general plant features.

Section A provides critical heat flux (CHF) test data and correlations for the NuScale fuel design, which are necessary inputs for the analytical models and parameter values referenced in Section B for evaluating plant response to various transient events.

Section A describes the plant-level infrastructure and utility systems (such as the Central Utility Building) that support module operations, while Section B specifies the analytical parameters and instrumentation setpoints used to evaluate module-level thermal-hydraulic responses to operational transients, with both sections connected through the reliance of module-level safety analyses on plant-level support systems as described in Table 1.2-1 and Table 5.1-2.

Section A and Section B are identical excerpts describing the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation and its main components, thus providing the same plant-level and module-level design information for comparison with tabulated data.

Section A provides a detailed module-level description of the reactor coolant system, which specifies and elaborates on the general plant-level systems overviewed in Section B, with both sections connected through design values and system boundaries referenced in Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, and Figure 5.1-2.

Section A provides a high-level summary of the reactor coolant system's design and function, while Section B offers detailed drawings and figures (such as P&IDs) that visually clarify and specify the components and configuration described in Section A, with both sections connected through references to specific tables and figures.

Section A specifies initial conditions and input parameters (such as initial power level and scram characteristics) for accident analysis scenarios, which must be consistent with the plant and module-level design basis and system descriptions provided in Section B, as referenced in the analysis question using Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, and Figure 5.1-2.

Section A specifies initial conditions and input parameters for accident analysis scenarios (such as initial power level and scram characteristics) that must be consistent with the general plant design and operating characteristics described in Section B, as referenced in Table 1.2-1.

Section A specifies initial conditions and input parameters (such as initial power level and scram characteristics) required for safety analysis, while Section B provides the detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify and support the understanding of these parameters and system configurations.

Section A specifies initial conditions and input parameters (such as core burnup, power level, and scram characteristics) for accident analysis scenarios, which must be consistent with the plant and module-level design basis descriptions of the reactor coolant system provided in Section B, as referenced in Table 1.2-1, Table 5.1-2, and Figure 5.1-2.

Section A specifies detailed input parameters and initial conditions for module-level thermal-hydraulic analyses, such as core burnup and scram characteristics, which are derived from or must be consistent with the general plant-level descriptions and design values provided in Section B.

Section A specifies initial conditions and input parameters for safety analysis scenarios at the plant level, while Section B provides detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and support the understanding of those plant-level parameters and configurations, with both sections connected through the use of referenced tables and figures (e.g., Table 1.2-1) to ensure consistency between descriptive and schematic information.

Section A provides a plant- and module-level design summary of the reactor coolant system, which establishes the physical and operational context for the initial conditions and input parameters (such as initial power level and scram characteristics) referenced in Section B's regulatory conformance analysis.

Section A provides a plant- and module-level design summary of the reactor coolant system, which establishes the physical and operational context for the input parameters and initial conditions referenced in Section B's regulatory conformance analysis for accident scenarios.

Section A and Section B both specify different input parameters (initial power level and core burnup, respectively) required for the thermal-hydraulic analysis of the Chemical and Volume Control System malfunction scenario, and together they define the necessary initial conditions for the analysis.
